title: Dog Limping After Falling Off Couch: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog limping after a fall? Learn why it happens, the urgency level, and how capturing photos or videos can assist your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog limping after a fall indicates pain and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours, or immediately if there are visible deformities or an inability to stand. Providing photos or videos of the gait can help your veterinary team quickly assess the severity of the injury.

Why is my dog limping after falling off the furniture?

It is incredibly stressful to watch your dog fall off the furniture and start limping. A limp is a physical sign of pain or discomfort, indicating that your dog is reluctant to put full weight on a limb. This could be caused by a simple muscle strain, a sprain, or more serious issues like a bone fracture or a joint luxation. Even a low-height fall from a couch can cause significant impact depending on how your dog landed.

How quickly does my limping dog need to see a veterinarian?

A dog limping after a fall is generally classified as a medium urgency situation. While it may not be a life-threatening emergency if your dog is acting normally otherwise, they are in pain and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours. You should proceed to an emergency clinic immediately if you see:

  • An open wound or visible bone
  • An obvious deformity in the leg
  • An inability to stand
  • Constant vocalizing or signs of extreme pain

How can I use photos and videos to help my vet triage my dog's limp?

When you contact your vet or an emergency clinic, providing visuals allows the veterinary team to see the degree of lameness and the specific leg affected. To help them prioritize your pet's care, try to:

  • Take a clear photo of the injured limb.
  • Record a short video of your dog attempting to walk.
  • Capture the video at your dog's eye level while they are on a non-slip surface, provided it is safe to do so.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following a fall from a couch, a dog exhibiting lameness requires a thorough physical examination, including palpation of the limbs from distal to proximal to identify abnormalities such as swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, or muscle atrophy. Radiography is a useful ancillary test to identify fractures or dislocations, and advanced imaging techniques like CT or MRI may be necessary for subtle lesions. The lameness examination should be performed with the animal at rest, rising, and during locomotion to assess single- or multiple-limb involvement and severity.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medicine like Tylenol or Ibuprofen?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human over-the-counter pain medications. Drugs like Ibuprofen, Naproxen, and Acetaminophen are toxic to dogs and can cause organ failure or severe gastric ulcers.

faqs.question: Should I encourage my dog to walk it off?
faqs.answer: No, you should strictly limit your dog's activity. Restrict them to a small area or a crate to prevent further injury until they can be examined by a professional.

faqs.question: How can I tell if the leg is broken?
faqs.answer: Signs of a fracture include a limb that looks a different shape than usual, rapid swelling, heat, or the inability to bear any weight at all. Only an X-ray can definitively confirm a break.
